content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA containing NNN positive integers.

You can compress this array by performing the following operation:


Choose any non-empty array BBB containing **positive integers**, such that there are **at least two disjoint occurrences** of BBB in AAA as a subarray†^\dagger†.
Then, choose two or more mutually disjoint occurrences of BBB among the subarrays of AAA.

Replace each such occurrence with the single integer −1-1−1.
For example, suppose A=[1,2,1,2,1,2,1,3,1,2,1]A = [1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 2, 1, 3, 1, 2, 1]A=[1,2,1,2,1,2,1,3,1,2,1]. Then,


If you choose B=[1,2,1]B = [1, 2, 1]B=[1,2,1], there are four occurrences of BBB in AAA, starting at indices 1,3,5,1, 3, 5, 1,3,5, and 999.

Choosing the occurrences at indices 1,5,91, 5, 91,5,9 to replace results in [−1,2,−1,3,−1][-1, 2, -1, 3, -1][−1,2,−1,3,−1].
Choosing the occurrences at indices 3,93, 93,9 to replace results in [1,2,−1,2,1,3,−1][1, 2, -1, 2, 1, 3, -1][1,2,−1,2,1,3,−1] instead.


On the other hand, choosing B=[1,2,1,2,1]B = [1, 2, 1, 2, 1]B=[1,2,1,2,1] is not valid: there are two occurrences of it in AAA (starting at indices 111 and 333), but it's not possible to find two disjoint occurrences.
Define f(A)f(A)f(A) to be the minimum possible length of AAA, if this compression operation can be performed however many times you like.

Find the minimum possible value of f(A)f(A)f(A) across all possible rearrangements of the given array AAA.

Note that you are not allowed to rearrange the array during the compression process itself: you can only choose the initial arrangement of elements.

†^\dagger† A subarray of AAA is any array that can be obtained by deleting some (possibly empty) prefix and some (possibly empty) suffix of AAA.

For example, if A=[1,2,1,3,5]A = [1, 2, 1, 3, 5]A=[1,2,1,3,5], some examples of its subarrays are [2,1,3],[2],[1,3],[2,1,3,5][2, 1, 3], [2], [1, 3], [2, 1, 3, 5][2,1,3],[2],[1,3],[2,1,3,5]. However, [1,5][1, 5][1,5] is not a subarray of AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing a single integer - the minimum possible value of f(A)f(A)f(A) across all rearrangements of AAA.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We can do the following:

Choose the rearrangement [1,2,5,6,1,2][1, 2, 5, 6, 1, 2][1,2,5,6,1,2].
Then, perform the compression operation with B=[1,2]B = [1, 2]B=[1,2].
The resulting array is [−1,5,6,−1][-1, 5, 6, -1][−1,5,6,−1], which has a length of 444.
It can be proved that obtaining a length smaller than 444 is impossible.

Test case 222: Consider the rearrangement [3,7,5,3,7,5,3,7,5][3, 7, 5, 3, 7, 5, 3, 7, 5][3,7,5,3,7,5,3,7,5]. With B=[3,7,5]B = [3, 7, 5]B=[3,7,5], the array can be compressed to [−1,−1,−1][-1, -1, -1][−1,−1,−1] which is the best we can do.
